title: CPI figures keep rate rises on table
full_text:
Headline inflation slowed to 4 per cent in May, assisted by the Albanese government's temporary fuel excise cut, but a rise in the Reserve Bank's preferred measure of price increases means further interest rate rises this year are still on the table.
The RBA's preferred measure of underlying inflation, which strips out the most volatile items, rose from 3.4 per cent to 3.6 per cent in May, data released by the Australian Bureau of Statistics on Wednesday showed.
The higher underlying inflation figure keeps alive the possibility of further monetary policy tightening by the RBA in the second half of the year.
RBA deputy governor Andrew Hauser said on Wednesday that the possible resolution to the Middle East conflict could help lower the global oil price and reduce fuel prices at home.
"But a full resolution is not yet assured, and we still have work to do to reduce inflation here in Australia, which remains far too high," Hauser said in a speech at the Economic Society of Australia.
The RBA's nine voting members unanimously held the cash rate at 4.35 per cent this month, pausing a cycle of three increases of 0.25 of a percentage point each this year.
"Unfortunately, today's data adds to the case for a rate hike," said KPMG chief economist Brendan Rynne.
"There are two numbers we look at - the most critical being core inflation - and frustratingly we are seeing this rise, which will give the RBA the justification it needs to increase rates, most likely in August.
"Beyond that, it's difficult to predict what will happen, but at least one more rise is guaranteed."
The 0.7 per cent monthly drop in the headline rate in May was driven by lower transportation costs arising from the government's temporary halving of the fuel excise. Other key monthly price declines were in clothing (down 2.9 per cent in the month) and recreation (down 3.1 per cent).
"This is the second consecutive month that we've seen headline inflation fall in our economy," Treasurer Jim Chalmers said. "Today's numbers are much better than what the market expected, and better than forecast.
"We are realistic, we're not complacent about the future trajectory of inflation, but you'd rather headline inflation coming down than going up."
The headline inflation rate has slowed since the government slashed the fuel excise tax to shield consumers from higher petrol prices arising from the conflict between Iran and the US.
The cost-of-living relief measure began on April 1, when bowser prices rocketed as the conflict choked global oil supplies that pass through the Strait of Hormuz. The strait has reopened under a shaky ceasefire agreement.
On Sunday, Labor extended the relief measure for another month, but at a lower rate. The temporary fuel excise cuts put downward pressure on inflation by forcing petrol prices lower, but added to demand by putting more money in the pockets of consumers.
Although underlying inflation was higher than the 3.5 per cent economists expected, headline inflation was lower than their prediction of 4.3 per cent.
The trimmed mean inflation rate strips out the most volatile price movements and is the RBA's preferred measure because it ignores one-off changes and temporary policy interventions.
Chalmers welcomed the drop in headline inflation, but did not say which of the two measurements he considered more important.
"We pay close attention to both measures of inflation and all the constituent parts of the inflation story," said Chalmers, when asked whether the government should be focusing, like the RBA, more on underlying figures.
He attributed the rise in trimmed mean inflation to the fuel crisis seeping into other parts of the economy.
Fairfax Media Management Pty Limited
author: Luke Kinsella
heading: Banking and Economy
